news_summary: Advancements in health research reveal promising breakthroughs in early heart disease detection, stress reduction through coffee, enhanced cancer immunotherapy, and understanding mysterious global phenomena.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès de la recherche en santé révèlent des avancées prometteuses dans le dépistage précoce des maladies cardiaques, la réduction du stress grâce au café, l'amélioration de l'immunothérapie contre le cancer et la compréhension de phénomènes mondiaux mystérieux.
news_summary_es: Los avances en la investigación sanitaria revelan descubrimientos prometedores en la detección precoz de las enfermedades cardíacas, la reducción del estrés gracias al café, la mejora de la inmunoterapia contra el cáncer y la comprensión de misteriosos fenómenos globales.

articles.title: New AI blood test predicts heart disease 15 years early
articles.summary: A new AI-powered blood test could give people a remarkably early warning of serious heart and circulation problems. Developed by researchers at the University of Hong Kong, CardiOmicScore analyzes thousands of proteins and metabolites to estimate the risk of six major cardiovascular diseases, including heart attack, stroke, heart failure, and atrial fibrillation. Unlike genetic risk scores, which remain fixed throughout life, the system captures biological changes linked to a person’s current health, lifestyle, and environment.
articles.content:
Researchers at the LKS Faculty of Medicine of the University of Hong Kong (HKUMed) have developed an artificial intelligence tool that may help predict serious cardiovascular problems many years before symptoms appear.

The system, known as CardiOmicScore, uses information from a single blood test to estimate a person's future risk of six major cardiovascular diseases (CVDs): coronary artery disease, stroke, heart failure, atrial fibrillation, peripheral artery disease and venous thromboembolism. In people at elevated risk, the model was able to detect warning signals as far as 15 years before clinical onset.

The findings were published in Nature Communications.

A Blood Test That Captures Current Health

Cardiovascular diseases remain the leading cause of death worldwide, accounting for approximately 19.8 million fatalities in 2022 alone.

Doctors commonly assess cardiovascular risk by examining factors such as age, blood pressure, smoking history and other standard clinical measurements. These indicators are useful, but they may not reveal the earliest biological changes taking place inside the body before a disease becomes apparent.

As a result, some people may not be identified as high risk until the best opportunity for prevention has already begun to narrow.

Genetic risk tests offer another way to estimate a person's likelihood of developing disease. Polygenic risk scores, for example, combine the effects of many genetic variants into a single measure of inherited risk. However, a person's genetic makeup is largely fixed at birth.

That means genetic scores cannot fully reflect more immediate changes caused by diet, exercise, aging, illness, environmental exposures or other influences on health.

CardiOmicScore was designed to provide a more current picture of what is happening inside the body.

AI Combines Thousands of Biological Signals

To build the tool, the HKUMed team used deep learning to combine several layers of biological information. This approach is known as multiomics because it brings together data from different areas of biology, including genomics, metabolomics and proteomics.

Genomics examines genetic information. Proteomics focuses on proteins, which carry out many essential functions in the body. Metabolomics studies small molecules called metabolites, which are produced as the body processes food, generates energy and responds to disease.

The researchers analyzed large scale population data from the UK Biobank. Their model examined 2,920 circulating proteins and 168 metabolites measured in blood samples.

Together, these molecules can provide a detailed snapshot of a person's current biological state. They may reflect subtle changes in immune activity, metabolism and vascular health before noticeable symptoms develop.

Professor Zhang Qingpeng, Associate Professor in the Department of Pharmacology and Pharmacy at HKUMed, explained, "Genes determine where we start -- they define our baseline health risk. However, proteins and metabolites reflect our current physical health. Our AI tool is designed to decode these complex molecular signals, enabling doctors and patients to identify risks much earlier, which can potentially change the trajectory of disease through timely lifestyle modifications and early prevention."

Predicting Six Cardiovascular Diseases

The results showed that CardiOmicScore could turn complex molecular measurements into personalized estimates of cardiovascular risk.

The system performed substantially better than conventional polygenic risk scores. Its accuracy improved further when researchers added clinical information such as age and gender.

The model was designed to assess the risk of coronary artery disease, stroke, heart failure, atrial fibrillation, peripheral artery disease and venous thromboembolism.

Atrial fibrillation is an irregular heartbeat that can increase the risk of stroke and other complications. Peripheral artery disease occurs when narrowed blood vessels reduce circulation to the limbs. Venous thromboembolism refers to dangerous blood clots that form in a vein and may travel to the lungs.

Among high risk individuals, CardiOmicScore could flag elevated cardiovascular risk up to 15 years before symptoms emerged.

Moving From Treatment to Earlier Prevention

The research reflects a broader shift in precision medicine.

Traditional genetic approaches provide a relatively fixed estimate of inherited risk. Multiomics tools may offer a more dynamic assessment by tracking biological signals that change over time.

In the future, a small blood sample could potentially be used to create a detailed risk profile covering several cardiovascular diseases at once. That information could give patients and doctors more time to respond with lifestyle changes, closer monitoring or other preventive measures.

Professor Zhang added, "We aim to leverage technology to identify and prevent diseases before they develop. By shifting health management from reactive treatment to proactive prediction and intervention, we aim to create a lasting impact for both public health and individual patient care."

About the Research Team

The study was led by Professor Zhang Qingpeng, Associate Professor in the Department of Pharmacology and Pharmacy, HKUMed, and the HKU Musketeers Foundation Institute of Data Science (IDS).

The first author is Luo Yan from the HKU IDS.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant medical breakthrough where an AI-powered blood test can predict six major cardiovascular diseases up to 15 years before symptoms appear. This early detection tool has broad implications for public health by enabling earlier prevention and intervention, potentially reducing the leading cause of death worldwide. The article provides detailed context on the technology, its advantages over existing methods, and the scope of diseases covered, fulfilling criteria for positive real-world impact, significance, and substance.
articles.personality_title: New AI blood test predicts heart disease risk up to 15 years early
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Heart disease and related problems like stroke are the leading causes of death worldwide. Doctors usually check risk by looking at age, blood pressure, and habits, but these methods may miss early signs of disease.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Hong Kong created an AI tool called CardiOmicScore that uses one blood test to predict the chance of six major heart and circulation diseases. The test looks at thousands of proteins and small molecules in the blood to understand a person’s current health. It can warn people up to 15 years before symptoms start.

**Impact** – This test is different from genetic tests because it shows changes happening right now in the body, not just inherited risk. It covers diseases like heart attack, stroke, heart failure, irregular heartbeat, and blood clots. By catching risks early, people can make lifestyle changes or get treatment sooner, which could prevent serious problems.

**What's next step** – Scientists hope this blood test will be used in hospitals and clinics to help doctors find risks earlier. It may lead to better health checkups and new ways to stop heart disease before it starts.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new AI blood test offers a detailed and early warning of heart disease risks, giving people more time to prevent serious illness.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau test sanguin IA prévoit le risque de maladie cardiaque jusqu’à 15 ans à l’avance
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les maladies cardiaques et les problèmes circulatoires sont les principales causes de décès dans le monde. Les médecins évaluent souvent le risque en regardant l’âge, la tension artérielle et les habitudes, mais ces méthodes peuvent manquer les premiers signes de maladie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Hong Kong ont créé un outil IA appelé CardiOmicScore qui utilise un test sanguin pour prédire le risque de six grandes maladies cardiovasculaires. Le test analyse des milliers de protéines et de petites molécules dans le sang pour comprendre la santé actuelle d’une personne. Il peut avertir jusqu’à 15 ans avant l’apparition des symptômes.

**Impact** – Ce test se distingue des tests génétiques car il montre les changements actuels dans le corps, pas seulement le risque hérité. Il couvre des maladies comme l’infarctus, l’AVC, l’insuffisance cardiaque, les battements irréguliers et les caillots sanguins. En détectant tôt les risques, les personnes peuvent changer leur mode de vie ou recevoir un traitement plus rapidement, ce qui peut prévenir de graves problèmes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques espèrent que ce test sanguin sera utilisé dans les hôpitaux pour aider les médecins à détecter les risques plus tôt. Cela pourrait améliorer les bilans de santé et aider à stopper les maladies cardiaques avant qu’elles ne commencent.

**Une phrase clé** – Un nouveau test sanguin IA offre un avertissement précis et précoce des risques cardiaques, donnant plus de temps pour prévenir des maladies graves.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo análisis de sangre con IA predice riesgo de enfermedad cardíaca hasta 15 años antes
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las enfermedades del corazón y problemas circulatorios son las principales causas de muerte en el mundo. Los médicos suelen evaluar el riesgo mirando la edad, la presión arterial y los hábitos, pero estos métodos pueden no detectar los primeros signos de la enfermedad.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Hong Kong crearon una herramienta de inteligencia artificial llamada CardiOmicScore que usa un análisis de sangre para predecir el riesgo de seis enfermedades cardiovasculares importantes. La prueba analiza miles de proteínas y pequeñas moléculas en la sangre para entender la salud actual de una persona. Puede avisar hasta 15 años antes de que aparezcan los síntomas.

**Impacto** – Esta prueba es diferente de las genéticas porque muestra los cambios que ocurren ahora en el cuerpo, no solo el riesgo heredado. Cubre enfermedades como ataque al corazón, derrame cerebral, insuficiencia cardíaca, latidos irregulares y coágulos. Al detectar el riesgo temprano, las personas pueden cambiar su estilo de vida o recibir tratamiento antes, lo que puede prevenir problemas graves.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos esperan que esta prueba de sangre se use en hospitales para ayudar a los doctores a encontrar riesgos antes. Esto podría mejorar los chequeos de salud y ayudar a detener las enfermedades cardíacas antes de que comiencen.

**Una frase clave** – Una nueva prueba de sangre con IA ofrece una advertencia detallada y temprana de riesgos cardíacos, dando más tiempo para prevenir enfermedades graves.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing human heart made of intricate, interwoven strands of DNA and delicate molecular structures like proteins and metabolites, surrounded by softly pulsing circuits and data streams symbolizing AI, set against a calm, natural background of gentle earth tones.

articles.title: Coffee may help the body fight stress and aging through a hidden cellular switch
articles.summary: Scientists may have uncovered one reason coffee is repeatedly associated with healthier aging and lower disease risk. Compounds in coffee appear to activate NR4A1, a receptor that helps protect cells from stress, inflammation, and damage. These protective effects disappeared when the receptor was removed, strengthening the connection. Surprisingly, plant-based compounds such as caffeic acid seemed far more active than caffeine itself.
articles.content:
Coffee has repeatedly been associated with longer life and a lower risk of several chronic illnesses. Even so, scientists have not fully understood the biological processes that might explain those benefits.

New findings from the Texas A&M College of Veterinary Medicine and Biomedical Sciences (VMBS) point to one possible answer. Researchers found that certain compounds in coffee may activate NR4A1, a receptor that is becoming increasingly important in studies of aging, stress responses and disease.

The research, recently published in Nutrients, offers one of the first direct links between coffee compounds and NR4A1. That connection may help explain some of the broad health effects associated with drinking coffee.

"Coffee has well-known health-promoting properties," said Dr. Stephen Safe, distinguished professor and Sid Kyle Endowed Chair in Veterinary Toxicology in VMBS' Department of Veterinary Physiology and Pharmacology. "What we've shown is that some of those effects may be linked to how coffee compounds interact with this receptor, which is involved in protecting the body from stress-induced damage."

How NR4A1 Helps Protect the Body

NR4A1 is part of a group of nuclear receptors that help control gene activity when the body is exposed to stress or tissue damage.

In earlier research, Safe and his collaborators described NR4A1 as a "nutrient sensor," meaning that it can respond to dietary compounds and contribute to the body's ability to remain healthy with age.

"If you damage almost any tissue, NR4A1 responds to bring that damage down," Safe said. "If you take that receptor away, the damage is worse."

Studies have connected NR4A1 with inflammation, metabolism and tissue repair. Each of these processes is closely involved in age-related conditions, including cancer, neurodegenerative disease and metabolic disorders.

A Possible Mechanism Behind Coffee's Benefits

Large observational studies have linked coffee consumption with a reduced risk of Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease and metabolic disease. However, those studies have generally shown associations rather than explaining exactly how coffee might produce protective effects.

Safe and his team proposed that NR4A1 could be one part of that explanation.

The project involved researchers from across Texas A&M, including Dr. Robert Chapkin, Dr. Roger Norton, Dr. James Cai and Dr. Shoshana Eitan. Their work helped show coffee's protective effects in neurological models.

The researchers found that several compounds in coffee can bind to NR4A1 and alter its activity. The most active included polyhydroxy and polyphenolic compounds such as caffeic acid.

"What we're saying is that at least part of coffee's health benefits may come through binding and activating this receptor," Safe said.

In laboratory models, these compounds also changed cell behavior in ways associated with disease protection. They reduced cellular damage and slowed the growth of cancer cells.

When the researchers removed NR4A1 from the cells, those protective effects disappeared. That result provided additional evidence that the receptor helps mediate at least some of coffee's biological effects.

Coffee's Benefits May Extend Beyond Caffeine

Caffeine is the largest individual component in coffee, but the study indicates that it may not be the main source of the beverage's protective effects.

Instead, naturally occurring compounds that are also present in many fruits and vegetables appeared to have a stronger influence on NR4A1.

"Caffeine binds the receptor, but it doesn't do much in our models," Safe said. "The polyhydroxy and polyphenolic compounds are much more active."

This finding may help explain why large population studies have linked both caffeinated and decaffeinated coffee with similar health benefits.

One Pathway Among Many

Safe cautioned that coffee is chemically complex and probably affects the body through multiple biological routes.

"There are many receptors and many mechanisms involved," he said. "What we're showing is that this could be one of the important pathways."

The study was designed to investigate biological mechanisms. It does not establish direct cause and effect in people or prove that drinking coffee prevents disease.

"There's still a lot of work to be done," Safe said. "We've made the connection, but we need to better understand how important that connection is."

The results support a growing body of research showing that diet, especially plant-based compounds, can influence biological pathways involved in aging and disease.

Because NR4A1 plays a role in several medical conditions, the findings may also contribute to future drug development. Safe's team is studying synthetic compounds that target the receptor more effectively than natural dietary substances, with the aim of developing possible treatments for cancer and other diseases.

The work also highlights the potential importance of routine dietary choices.

"Coffee is a very complex mixture of compounds," Safe said. "It's a very potent combination."

What the Findings Mean for Coffee Drinkers

The research does not change current recommendations for coffee consumption. People can also respond differently depending on their health, sensitivity to caffeine and other individual factors.

Still, the findings provide scientists with something that has been difficult to identify: a possible biological explanation for coffee's long-standing association with better health and longevity.

"I think it helps explain why coffee has the effects that it does," Safe said. "It's not just an observation -- there's a mechanism behind it."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery linking coffee compounds to activation of a receptor (NR4A1) that protects cells from stress, inflammation, and damage, potentially explaining coffee's association with healthier aging and reduced disease risk. This finding has broad implications for public health and future medical research, including drug development, and provides meaningful insight into diet-related disease prevention.
articles.personality_title: Coffee compounds may activate a key receptor that helps protect cells from stress and aging
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Coffee has long been linked to longer life and lower risk of diseases like Alzheimer’s and cancer. However, scientists have not fully understood how coffee helps the body stay healthy.

**What happened** – Researchers at Texas A&M discovered that certain compounds in coffee can activate a receptor called NR4A1. This receptor helps protect cells from stress, inflammation, and damage. When the receptor was removed in lab tests, the protective effects disappeared. The study found that plant-based compounds like caffeic acid were more active than caffeine in activating NR4A1.

**Impact** – This discovery provides a clear biological reason why coffee may support healthier aging and reduce disease risk. It shows that coffee’s benefits come from complex natural compounds, not just caffeine. Understanding NR4A1’s role could also help develop new medicines for diseases related to aging and inflammation.

**What's next step** – Scientists will continue studying how strongly coffee affects NR4A1 in people and explore synthetic compounds that target this receptor even better. This research might lead to new treatments for cancer and other diseases.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Compounds in coffee activate a cellular receptor that helps protect the body from stress and aging, offering a scientific explanation for coffee’s health benefits.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les composés du café pourraient activer un récepteur clé qui protège les cellules du stress et du vieillissement
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le café est depuis longtemps associé à une vie plus longue et à un risque réduit de maladies comme Alzheimer et le cancer. Pourtant, les scientifiques ne comprenaient pas exactement comment le café aide le corps à rester en bonne santé.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de Texas A&M ont découvert que certains composés du café peuvent activer un récepteur appelé NR4A1. Ce récepteur aide à protéger les cellules contre le stress, l’inflammation et les dommages. Lorsque ce récepteur a été retiré lors de tests en laboratoire, les effets protecteurs ont disparu. L’étude a montré que des composés d’origine végétale comme l’acide caféique étaient plus actifs que la caféine pour activer NR4A1.

**Impact** – Cette découverte donne une raison biologique claire expliquant pourquoi le café peut favoriser un vieillissement en meilleure santé et réduire les risques de maladies. Elle montre que les bienfaits du café viennent de composés naturels complexes, pas seulement de la caféine. Comprendre le rôle de NR4A1 pourrait aussi aider à développer de nouveaux médicaments pour des maladies liées au vieillissement et à l’inflammation.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont continuer à étudier l’effet du café sur NR4A1 chez l’humain et explorer des composés synthétiques ciblant ce récepteur de manière plus efficace. Cette recherche pourrait conduire à de nouveaux traitements contre le cancer et d’autres maladies.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Des composés du café activent un récepteur cellulaire qui aide à protéger le corps contre le stress et le vieillissement, offrant une explication scientifique aux bienfaits du café.
articles.personality_title_es: Los compuestos del café pueden activar un receptor clave que protege las células del estrés y el envejecimiento
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El café ha estado vinculado durante mucho tiempo a una vida más larga y a un menor riesgo de enfermedades como el Alzheimer y el cáncer. Sin embargo, los científicos no entendían bien cómo el café ayuda al cuerpo a mantenerse saludable.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de Texas A&M descubrieron que ciertos compuestos del café pueden activar un receptor llamado NR4A1. Este receptor ayuda a proteger las células del estrés, la inflamación y el daño. Cuando el receptor fue eliminado en pruebas de laboratorio, los efectos protectores desaparecieron. El estudio encontró que compuestos vegetales como el ácido cafeico eran más activos que la cafeína para activar NR4A1.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo ofrece una razón biológica clara de por qué el café puede apoyar un envejecimiento más saludable y reducir el riesgo de enfermedades. Muestra que los beneficios del café provienen de compuestos naturales complejos, no solo de la cafeína. Entender el papel de NR4A1 también podría ayudar a desarrollar nuevos medicamentos para enfermedades relacionadas con el envejecimiento y la inflamación.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán estudiando cómo afecta el café a NR4A1 en las personas y explorarán compuestos sintéticos que puedan activar este receptor aún mejor. Esta investigación podría conducir a nuevos tratamientos para el cáncer y otras enfermedades.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los compuestos del café activan un receptor celular que ayuda a proteger el cuerpo del estrés y el envejecimiento, proporcionando una explicación científica a los beneficios del café.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a steaming cup of coffee whose swirling vapor gently transforms into a glowing, intricate molecular structure symbolizing the NR4A1 receptor, set against a softly lit abstract background of cellular shapes and subtle plant-like polyphenol motifs in natural, earthy tones.

articles.title: Could this popular gym supplement strengthen cancer immunotherapy?
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM Cancer immunotherapy has transformed treatment for some patients. For the 60 to 80 percent who don’t respond, the problem often isn’t the killer T cells at the end of the immune chain. It’s the cells responsible for training them. A new UCLA study published in iScience identifies creatine, the […]
The post Could this popular gym supplement strengthen cancer immunotherapy? first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
Cancer immunotherapy has transformed treatment for some patients. For the 60 to 80 percent who don’t respond, the problem often isn’t the killer T cells at the end of the immune chain. It’s the cells responsible for training them. A new UCLA study published in iScience identifies creatine, the supplement commonly used by athletes and bodybuilders, as a critical fuel source for those training cells, and finds that supplementing with it significantly slowed tumor growth in mouse models.

Why dendritic cells are the missing link in immunotherapy

Killer T cells are the immune system’s frontline fighters against cancer. But before they can act, dendritic cells must capture fragments from tumors, carry those fragments to T cells, and direct them toward the target. Most approved cancer immunotherapies skip this step and go straight for T cells. That may be why so many patients don’t respond.

The UCLA team, whose earlier work showed creatine fuels killer T cells in tumor environments, turned its attention to dendritic cells after noticing something in the metabolic data. When the researchers looked at which genes were most active in dendritic cells that had infiltrated tumors in mice, one stood out: the gene for the creatine transporter, a protein that pulls creatine into cells, was significantly elevated in tumor-infiltrating dendritic cells compared to those in healthy tissue.

What creatine deprivation and supplementation revealed

To find out what that meant, the team removed the creatine transporter from dendritic cells entirely. Those cells showed impaired survival, reduced activation, and a weakened ability to prime T cells for a tumor response. When creatine-deficient dendritic cells were grown alongside T cells in the lab, the T cells divided less and produced fewer of the signaling molecules cancer-fighting requires.

The team then ran the opposite experiment. Daily creatine injections in mouse models of melanoma significantly slowed tumor growth, boosted the number of dendritic cells inside tumors, and increased their activation. Creatine-treated dendritic cells also produced more of the chemical signals that draw additional immune cells into the tumor microenvironment.

Metabolomics analyses pointed to the mechanism: creatine raised intracellular ATP levels in dendritic cells. ATP is what cells use to power nearly every function. Creatine acts like a battery, releasing stored energy when demand spikes, which helps dendritic cells hold their metabolic position even while competing with fast-growing tumor cells for nutrients. The inflammatory signaling pathways needed for activation stay on.

“What this study shows is that creatine doesn’t just help the T cells fighting cancer — it also energizes the entire infrastructure that supports and guides them,” said Lili Yang, the study’s senior author and a professor of microbiology, immunology and molecular genetics at UCLA. “That makes creatine a promising supplement to holistically support the immune response that modern immunotherapies depend on.”

Human cell results and implications for cancer vaccines

The same pattern held in human cell experiments. Creatine enhanced the activation of human monocyte-derived dendritic cells, a type commonly used to make dendritic cell cancer vaccines, and improved their ability to stimulate human T cells against a cancer-associated target. The team suggests incorporating creatine into the manufacturing process for those vaccines could increase their potency before a patient ever receives them.

Co-first author James Elsten-Brown described the two directions the research points toward: “creatine could be used in two complementary ways: as a supplement to enhance the immune response of patients already receiving immunotherapy, and as a tool to improve the quality of dendritic cell-based vaccines before they’re administered.”

Clinical outlook and important caveats for patients

This research was done in mouse models and human cells, not in patients. No clinical recommendations follow from it, and anyone undergoing cancer treatment should check with their physician before adding any supplement to their routine.

Creatine monohydrate has been used as a supplement for decades and is generally regarded as safe at recommended doses. Because that safety profile is already established, the bar for clinical testing is lower than it would be for a new drug. The team is working with physicians toward prospective trials to test whether creatine supplementation improves outcomes in patients receiving immunotherapy.

“Understanding how to metabolically support dendritic cells is about supporting the entire anti-tumor response, not just the killer T cells at the end of it,” said co-first author Elliot Kang. Rather than layering another intervention onto T cells directly, this approach works upstream. If the results translate to humans, creatine could turn out to be one of the least expensive components in an immunotherapy protocol.

Source study: iScience – Creatine uptake promotes dendritic cell activation and enhances antitumor immunity
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant scientific discovery that creatine supplementation can enhance the effectiveness of cancer immunotherapy by energizing dendritic cells, which are crucial for activating T cells to fight tumors. This finding has broad potential impact for cancer treatment, offering a promising, low-cost adjunct to existing therapies. The study is detailed, focused on a single breakthrough, and supported by experimental evidence in mouse models and human cells, indicating meaningful progress in cancer immunotherapy.
articles.personality_title: Creatine supplement may boost cancer immunotherapy by energizing key immune cells
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Cancer immunotherapy helps the immune system fight cancer, but it only works well for about 20 to 40 percent of patients. One reason many patients don’t respond is that the immune cells called dendritic cells, which train killer T cells to attack tumors, don’t work properly.

**What happened** – Researchers at UCLA studied how creatine, a popular gym supplement, affects dendritic cells. They found that dendritic cells need creatine to survive and activate T cells against cancer. In experiments with mice, giving creatine slowed tumor growth and helped dendritic cells work better. Tests with human cells showed creatine also improved immune cell activation.

**Impact** – This study reveals that creatine supports not just the killer T cells but the whole system that trains them. Because creatine is already known to be safe and inexpensive, it could become an easy way to improve cancer treatments. Also, adding creatine to cancer vaccines made from dendritic cells might make those vaccines stronger.

**What's next step** – The researchers plan to test creatine supplements in clinical trials with cancer patients receiving immunotherapy. They want to see if creatine can help patients respond better to treatment. However, patients should not take creatine without talking to their doctor first.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Creatine, a common supplement, might boost cancer immunotherapy by helping dendritic cells activate killer T cells to fight tumors more effectively.
articles.personality_title_fr: La créatine pourrait renforcer l’immunothérapie contre le cancer en stimulant des cellules clés du système immunitaire
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’immunothérapie du cancer aide le système immunitaire à combattre le cancer, mais elle fonctionne bien seulement pour 20 à 40 % des patients. Une des raisons est que les cellules dendritiques, qui entraînent les cellules T tueuses à attaquer les tumeurs, ne fonctionnent pas correctement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’UCLA ont étudié l’effet de la créatine, un complément populaire en salle de sport, sur les cellules dendritiques. Ils ont découvert que ces cellules ont besoin de créatine pour survivre et activer les cellules T contre le cancer. Chez des souris, la créatine a ralenti la croissance des tumeurs et amélioré le fonctionnement des cellules dendritiques. Des tests sur des cellules humaines ont montré que la créatine améliore aussi l’activation des cellules immunitaires.

**Impact** – Cette étude montre que la créatine soutient non seulement les cellules T tueuses, mais tout le système qui les forme. Comme la créatine est déjà reconnue comme sûre et peu coûteuse, elle pourrait facilement améliorer les traitements contre le cancer. Ajouter de la créatine aux vaccins anticancéreux à base de cellules dendritiques pourrait aussi renforcer leur efficacité.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient des essais cliniques pour tester la créatine chez des patients recevant une immunothérapie. Ils veulent vérifier si la créatine améliore la réponse au traitement. Cependant, les patients doivent consulter leur médecin avant de prendre de la créatine.

**En résumé** – La créatine, un complément courant, pourrait renforcer l’immunothérapie contre le cancer en aidant les cellules dendritiques à activer plus efficacement les cellules T tueuses.
articles.personality_title_es: La creatina podría fortalecer la inmunoterapia contra el cáncer al energizar células inmunitarias clave
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La inmunoterapia contra el cáncer ayuda al sistema inmunitario a combatir el cáncer, pero solo funciona bien en el 20 a 40 por ciento de los pacientes. Una razón es que las células dendríticas, que entrenan a las células T asesinas para atacar tumores, no funcionan correctamente.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de UCLA estudiaron cómo la creatina, un suplemento popular en gimnasios, afecta a las células dendríticas. Descubrieron que estas células necesitan creatina para sobrevivir y activar las células T contra el cáncer. En experimentos con ratones, la creatina ralentizó el crecimiento de tumores y mejoró el trabajo de las células dendríticas. Pruebas con células humanas también mostraron que la creatina mejora la activación inmunitaria.

**Impacto** – Este estudio muestra que la creatina apoya no solo a las células T asesinas, sino a todo el sistema que las entrena. Como la creatina es segura y barata, podría ser una forma sencilla de mejorar los tratamientos contra el cáncer. Además, añadir creatina a las vacunas de células dendríticas podría hacerlas más efectivas.

**Siguiente paso** – Los investigadores planean ensayos clínicos para probar la creatina en pacientes que reciben inmunoterapia. Quieren ver si la creatina mejora la respuesta al tratamiento. Sin embargo, los pacientes deben consultar a su médico antes de tomar creatina.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La creatina, un suplemento común, podría mejorar la inmunoterapia contra el cáncer al ayudar a las células dendríticas a activar mejor a las células T asesinas para combatir tumores.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing, stylized battery-shaped cell energizing a group of abstract, shield-like dendritic cells that are guiding dynamic, shielded T-cell silhouettes forward against a soft, natural background of interconnected cellular networks, using gentle earth tones and muted greens.

articles.title: The mysterious hum heard around the world may finally have an explanation
articles.summary: The mysterious sound known as The Hum has been reported worldwide for decades, yet its source often remains impossible to find. Researchers tested whether people who hear it have exceptional low-frequency hearing or measurable sounds produced inside their ears, but neither explanation fit most participants. The strongest possibility is that many cases involve low-frequency tinnitus, even though the sound seems completely external.
articles.content:
Do you sometimes notice a deep buzzing or humming noise that seems to have no identifiable source? If so, you may be among the estimated 2-4 percent of people worldwide who experience this unusual sound. Researchers have spent decades trying to determine where it originates.

For some, the noise is irritating but manageable. For others, the low-frequency sound can cause physical discomfort or illness and may also feel like a vibration moving through the body.

The hum can be difficult to detect outdoors. It is more commonly noticed inside buildings, especially at night when people are trying to sleep. Someone may look outside for a nearby engine or machine, only to find nothing that could explain the noise.

Even more puzzling, other people in the same room may hear nothing at all.

Reports of The Hum Begin in England

The phenomenon first gained widespread attention in Bristol, England, during the mid-1970s. The Bristol Evening Post began receiving a stream of letters from residents who described hearing an unexplained sound and wanted to know what was producing it.

One proposed explanation pointed to large industrial fans operating inside a department store warehouse. Yet the reports continued even after the warehouse closed several years later.

Similar accounts later emerged elsewhere in the United Kingdom, particularly in coastal communities including Hythe, Plymouth, Southampton and Swansea. Reports also came from London.

The mysterious sound became known as The Hum phenomenon, or simply The Hum.

During the 1990s, reports began appearing in the United States, initially in Taos, New Mexico and Kokomo, Indiana. The phenomenon has since been documented in Canada, Australia, New Zealand, South Africa and several European cities. Most reports tend to come from relatively densely populated areas.

According to the Norwegian Broadcasting Corporation (NRK), residents around Oslo also reported an unexplained humming noise a couple of years ago.

Canadian teacher Glen MacPherson first heard The Hum while living and working on the west coast of Canada. After moving to another city in the same region, he could no longer hear it.

His curiosity eventually led him to establish the interactive The World Hum Map and Database Project in 2012. The project gathers reports and location data from people who say they have experienced the sound.

Possible Sources Range From Machines to Nature

Researchers and the public have proposed numerous explanations for The Hum. Suggested causes include acoustic pollution from human activity, naturally occurring environmental sounds and conspiracy theories involving the CIA or even aliens.

Many technologies can generate low-frequency noise, including ventilation systems, heat pumps, road traffic and windmills. Nature also produces sounds in this range through sources such as ocean waves striking the shore and wind moving across the landscape.

The mystery has drawn the attention of hearing specialists and audiology researchers around the world. One of them is Markus Drexl, a professor at the Norwegian University of Science and Technology (NTNU).

Drexl, two PhD research fellows and a postdoc studied 28 people in Germany who reported hearing an unexplained hum or buzz.

Testing Whether the Hum Comes From Outside

The researchers examined two main hypotheses.

The first was that The Hum might be an externally produced sound that could be measured. Such noise could come from industry, infrastructure or natural processes that generate low-frequency waves.

"We know that there are people who hear low-frequency sounds that can actually be measured, even if other people don't hear them. But it's not so easy to find the source of these sound waves, because it's a struggle to localize low-frequency sounds," Drexl said.

Low-frequency sound waves have long wavelengths, which allows them to travel across considerable distances. That makes their source especially difficult to pinpoint.

Most Participants Did Not Have Exceptional Hearing

The team first investigated whether the participants were unusually sensitive to known low-frequency sounds.

Most showed no exceptional ability in this range. Only two people had better than average hearing at certain low frequencies.

"Even though the group we tested was small, it still means that the hypothesis of having especially good hearing for low-frequency sounds does not hold for most people," Drexl said.

However, he noted an important limitation. Tiny variations in hearing thresholds (microstructures) may allow some people to detect sound within an extremely narrow frequency range, such as between 50 and 51 Hertz. Standard hearing tests are not designed to capture differences this precise.

Could the Inner Ear Be Producing the Noise?

The cochlea inside the inner ear naturally creates faint sounds at different frequencies, generally between about 500 and 5000 Hertz. These noises do not serve a direct purpose. Instead, they are a by-product of the ear's physiological sound amplification process.

"Most of us don't hear these sounds. However, a few people can actually hear the sounds that the ear itself produces. And these sounds can be measured objectively," Drexl said.

Known as oto-acoustic emissions, these sounds can be detected by placing a sensitive microphone inside the ear canal. In certain individuals, spontaneous oto-acoustic emissions may be perceived as distressing tinnitus.

"One hypothesis was that the participants in our group could hear oto-acoustic emissions at low frequencies. That's why we tested whether they had them," says Drexl.

But… the answer was no.

Low-Frequency Tinnitus May Explain Many Cases

"Then there are people who hear something that cannot be measured objectively. We believe people in this category have a form of low-frequency tinnitus," Drexl said.

Tinnitus, often described as ringing in the ears, occurs when someone perceives a sound inside the ear or head even though no external source is producing it.

Tinnitus may be temporary or persistent. People often initially interpret the sound as something coming from their surroundings.

When the noise continues after they change locations, however, they may eventually realize that it is not being generated by anything nearby.

Based on current knowledge of hearing and the results of the participant tests, Drexl believes the most likely explanation has two parts.

A small number of people who hear The Hum may genuinely have unusually sensitive low-frequency hearing. For most, however, the experience may be a type of tinnitus in which the sound begins within the auditory system.

"Based on our results, although we haven't ruled out cases of physical external sound sources, we suggest that subjective tinnitus in the low-frequency range is often the cause of hearing pulsations of low-frequency sound perceptions," he said.

Scientists Need a Better Picture of Low-Frequency Hearing

Drexl became interested in The Hum through his research into low-frequency sound.

"What we know about the hearing system is mainly based on how we capture and process sound with higher frequencies. We know less about how the auditory system handles and processes low-frequency sound, or infrasound," he said.

Concern about noise from technological sources has increased during the past decade, particularly for sounds in the low-frequency range (between about 20 and 250 Hz) and infrasound (below 20 Hz).

"If we want to conduct a thorough assessment of low-frequency sounds and infrasound, we first need a better understanding of how sensory systems process low-frequency sound and infrasound," he said.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on scientific research providing a plausible explanation for a mysterious auditory phenomenon affecting 2-4% of the global population. The findings improve understanding of low-frequency tinnitus, a condition impacting many people's quality of life, and highlight the need for further study of low-frequency hearing. This represents a meaningful advancement in health science with broad societal relevance.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find new clues to explain the mysterious worldwide hum
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For decades, people around the world have reported hearing a strange, low buzzing noise called The Hum. This sound often seems to come from nowhere and is only heard by some people, usually indoors and at night. The source of The Hum has remained a mystery despite many theories.

**What happened** – Researchers in Germany studied 28 individuals who said they heard The Hum. They tested if these people had unusually sharp hearing for low sounds or if their ears made the noise themselves. The tests showed most did not have special hearing, and the noise was not coming from inside their ears. Instead, the researchers think many people are experiencing a type of low-frequency tinnitus, a condition where the brain hears sounds that are not actually present outside.

**Impact** – This study helps explain why The Hum is so hard to find and why only some people hear it. It shows that for most, The Hum may not be caused by machines or nature but by the way their own hearing works. Understanding low-frequency tinnitus better can improve how doctors help people who struggle with these mysterious sounds.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to learn more about how the ear and brain process low-frequency sounds. This could lead to better tests and treatments for people who hear The Hum or have tinnitus. More research is needed to fully understand why some people experience these sounds and how to reduce their impact.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New research suggests the mysterious Hum heard worldwide is often a form of low-frequency tinnitus rather than an external noise source.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent de nouvelles pistes pour expliquer le bourdonnement mystérieux mondial
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis des décennies, des personnes dans le monde entier entendent un étrange bourdonnement grave appelé Le Bourdonnement. Ce son semble souvent venir de nulle part et n’est entendu que par certaines personnes, généralement à l’intérieur et la nuit. L’origine du Bourdonnement est restée un mystère malgré de nombreuses théories.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs en Allemagne ont étudié 28 personnes affirmant entendre Le Bourdonnement. Ils ont testé si ces personnes avaient une audition particulièrement fine pour les sons graves ou si leurs oreilles produisaient elles-mêmes le bruit. Les tests ont montré que la plupart n’avaient pas d’audition spéciale et que le bruit ne venait pas de leurs oreilles. Les chercheurs pensent donc que beaucoup vivent un type d’acouphène basse fréquence, un phénomène où le cerveau perçoit des sons qui ne sont pas réellement présents à l’extérieur.

**Impact** – Cette étude aide à comprendre pourquoi Le Bourdonnement est si difficile à localiser et pourquoi seules certaines personnes l’entendent. Elle montre que pour la plupart, Le Bourdonnement n’est pas causé par des machines ou la nature, mais par le fonctionnement de leur propre audition. Mieux comprendre les acouphènes basse fréquence peut améliorer la prise en charge médicale.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent approfondir leurs connaissances sur la manière dont l’oreille et le cerveau traitent les sons graves. Cela pourrait conduire à de meilleurs tests et traitements pour les personnes qui entendent Le Bourdonnement ou souffrent d’acouphènes. Davantage de recherches sont nécessaires pour savoir pourquoi certaines personnes perçoivent ces sons et comment réduire leur impact.

**En une phrase** – Une nouvelle recherche suggère que le mystérieux Bourdonnement entendu dans le monde est souvent une forme d’acouphène basse fréquence plutôt qu’un son extérieur.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos encuentran nuevas pistas para explicar el zumbido misterioso mundial
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante décadas, personas en todo el mundo han reportado escuchar un extraño zumbido grave llamado El Zumbido. Este sonido parece no venir de ningún lugar y solo lo escuchan algunas personas, generalmente dentro de edificios y por la noche. El origen de El Zumbido ha sido un misterio a pesar de muchas teorías.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores en Alemania estudiaron a 28 personas que dijeron escuchar El Zumbido. Evaluaron si tenían una audición especialmente aguda para sonidos bajos o si sus oídos producían el ruido. Las pruebas mostraron que la mayoría no tenía una audición especial y que el ruido no provenía de sus oídos. En cambio, los investigadores creen que muchas personas experimentan un tipo de tinnitus de baja frecuencia, una condición donde el cerebro oye sonidos que no existen afuera.

**Impacto** – Este estudio ayuda a explicar por qué El Zumbido es difícil de localizar y por qué solo algunas personas lo escuchan. Muestra que para la mayoría, El Zumbido no es causado por máquinas o la naturaleza, sino por cómo funciona su propia audición. Entender mejor el tinnitus de baja frecuencia puede mejorar la ayuda médica para quienes sufren estos sonidos.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren aprender más sobre cómo el oído y el cerebro procesan los sonidos de baja frecuencia. Esto podría llevar a mejores pruebas y tratamientos para quienes escuchan El Zumbido o tienen tinnitus. Se necesita más investigación para entender por qué algunas personas experimentan estos sonidos y cómo reducir su impacto.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Nuevas investigaciones sugieren que el misterioso Zumbido escuchado en todo el mundo es a menudo una forma de tinnitus de baja frecuencia y no un ruido externo.
articles.image_prompt: A serene nighttime indoor scene with softly glowing sound waves gently flowing through a quiet room, subtly vibrating the air and surrounding objects, while a stylized abstract ear silhouette listens attentively, all rendered in warm, muted earth tones and delicate brushstrokes.
